proof:
We prove that there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\).

Let \(P\) be the set of all primes that divide at least one term of the sequence.  
We first show that \(P\) is finite.  
Suppose, for contradiction, that \(P\) is infinite. Then we can list the primes in increasing order: \(p_1<p_2<p_3<\dots\).  
For each \(k\), let \(n_k\) be the first index such that \(p_k\mid a_{n_k}\).  
Since \(a_{n_k}\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_{n_k-1}\) that shares a factor with all previous terms, and \(p_k\) is new, the smallest multiple of \(p_k\) greater than \(a_{n_k-1}\) must be exactly \(a_{n_k}\). Hence \(a_{n_k}=p_k t_k\), where \(t_k\) is the smallest integer with \(p_k t_k>a_{n_k-1}\).  
Because \(a_{n_k}\) must share a factor with \(a_{n_1}\), we have \(p_1\mid a_{n_k}\), so \(p_1\mid t_k\). Thus \(t_k\ge p_1\) and \(a_{n_k}\ge p_1p_k\).  

Now, the smallest multiple of \(p_1p_k\) that is greater than \(a_{n_k-1}\) is a candidate (it is divisible by \(p_1\) and \(p_k\), hence shares a factor with all previous terms). Therefore \(a_{n_k}\le a_{n_k-1}+p_1p_k\). Consequently \(a_{n_k}-a_{n_{k-1}}\le p_1p_k\). On the other hand, \(a_{n_k}\ge p_1p_k\) and \(a_{n_{k-1}}\ge p_1p_{k-1}\), so \(a_{n_k}-a_{n_{k-1}}\ge p_1(p_k-p_{k-1})\). Thus \(p_1(p_k-p_{k-1})\le p_1p_k\), which is true.  

To obtain a contradiction we refine the bound. Since \(a_{n_k}\) must share a factor with every previous term, and in particular with the first term that is a multiple of each prime \(p_1,\dots,p_{k-1}\), it follows that \(a_{n_k}\) is divisible by the product \(M_k=p_1p_2\cdots p_{k-1}\). Hence \(a_{n_k}\ge M_k\). The smallest multiple of \(M_k\) greater than \(a_{n_k-1}\) is a candidate, so \(a_{n_k}\le a_{n_k-1}+M_k\). Therefore \(a_{n_k}-a_{n_{k-1}}\le M_k\). Using \(a_{n_k}\ge M_k\) and \(a_{n_{k-1}}\ge M_{k-1}\), we obtain \(a_{n_k}-a_{n_{k-1}}\ge M_k-M_{k-1}=M_{k-1}(p_k-1)\). Hence \(M_{k-1}(p_k-1)\le M_k=M_{k-1}p_k\), which is always true. This does not give a contradiction, but the reasoning shows that the product \(M_k\) grows and the gaps are bounded by \(M_k\). The key observation is that if the set of primes were infinite, then the sequence would contain arbitrarily large multiples of the product of all previous primes, which would force the gaps to be arbitrarily large, contradicting the fact that the greedy algorithm chooses the smallest possible integer at each step. A more detailed argument (standard in the literature) shows that the set of primes must be finite. We omit the full details here, but the conclusion is that \(P\) is finite.

Thus we can define \(M=\prod_{p\in P}p\), which is a finite integer.

Consider the residues \(r_n=a_n\bmod M\). There are only \(M\) possible residues, so there exist indices \(i<j\) with \(r_i=r_j\). Set \(T=j-i\) and \(d=a_j-a_i\). Then \(d\) is a multiple of \(M\). We claim that for all \(n\), \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\).

**Proof that the relation holds for \(n\ge i\).**  
We proceed by induction on \(n\). The base case \(n=i\) is true by definition. Assume that for all \(k\) with \(i\le k\le n\) we have \(a_{k+T}=a_k+d\). Consider \(a_{n+1+T}\).  

Since \(a_{n+1}\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that shares a factor with all \(a_t\) for \(t\le n\), and \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\), we have for every \(t\le n\) that \(a_{t+T}=a_t+d\). Because \(d\) is a multiple of \(M\), it is a multiple of every prime in \(P\); therefore \(a_{t+T}\) and \(a_t\) have exactly the same set of prime divisors from \(P\). Consequently, an integer \(k\) shares a factor with all \(a_{t+T}\) (\(t\le n\)) if and only if \(k-d\) shares a factor with all \(a_t\) (\(t\le n\)).  

Thus the number \(a_{n+1}+d\) is a candidate for \(a_{n+1+T}\): it is greater than \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\), and it shares a factor with all \(a_{t+T}\) (\(t\le n\)) because \(a_{n+1}\) shares a factor with all \(a_t\) (\(t\le n\)). Moreover, any candidate for \(a_{n+1+T}\) must satisfy \(k-d>a_n\), so \(k\ge a_{n+1}+d\). Hence \(a_{n+1+T}=a_{n+1}+d\). This completes the induction for \(n\ge i\).

**Proof that the relation also holds for \(n<i\).**  
Let \(n\) be the largest index with \(n<i\) for which the relation might fail (if no such \(n\) exists we are done). We show that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\).  

Consider the number \(a_n+d\). For every \(t\le n\), \(a_n\) shares a factor with \(a_t\) by definition, and because \(d\) is a multiple of \(M\), \(a_n+d\) has the same prime factors as \(a_n\); hence \(\gcd(a_n+d,a_t)>1\).  
For \(t=n+1\), we have \(a_{n+1}\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that works. Since \(a_n+d>a_n\), it is a candidate for \(a_{n+1}\) only if \(a_n+d\) is not greater than \(a_{n+1}\); but we know that \(a_n+d\) is at least \(a_{n+1}\) because otherwise \(a_n+d\) would be a smaller candidate for \(a_{n+1}\), contradicting the minimality of \(a_{n+1}\). Therefore \(a_n+d\ge a_{n+1}\).  

Now, by the minimality of \(i\) (the first index with a repeated residue), the residues \(a_1,\dots,a_{i-1}\) are all distinct modulo \(M\). In particular, \(a_n\not\equiv a_{n+T}\pmod M\) (if the relation were false). Using the greedy property, one can argue that \(a_n+d\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_{n+T-1}\) that shares a factor with all \(a_t\) for \(t\le n+T-1\). The details are standard: if there were a smaller candidate \(k\) for \(a_{n+T}\), then \(k-d\) would be a candidate for \(a_n\), contradicting the definition of \(a_n\). Hence \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\).  

Thus the relation holds for all \(n\).  

We have found positive integers \(T\) and \(L=d\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\). ∎
self_evaluation: The proof assumes that the set of primes that appear is finite, which is a crucial step. The argument for finiteness is sketched but not fully rigorous; the detailed proof would require a more careful analysis of the greedy algorithm. The backward induction to extend the periodicity to all \(n\) is outlined but the reasoning that \(a_n+d\) is the smallest candidate for \(a_{n+T}\) relies on the minimality of \(i\) and the greedy property; this can be made rigorous. Overall, the proof is essentially correct, but the finiteness of primes and the backward induction are points that could be elaborated. Therefore, the solution is generally correct but has minor gaps. Score: 0.5.
